event: Archaic Age peoples, among the earliest known inhabitants, settled on the island that would become Grenada.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid migrants from South America established agricultural villages and ceramic-making communities on the island.
Later Arawak (Igneri) populations expanded across the island, developing pottery, fishing, and cassava cultivation.
Island-Carib (Kalinago) peoples migrated to the island, displacing or absorbing earlier Arawak inhabitants and calling it Camerhogne.
Christopher Columbus sighted the island during his third voyage and named it Concepcion.
An English settlement attempt failed when the colonists were driven off by the resisting Kalinago inhabitants.
French settlers led by Governor Du Parquet of Martinique purchased and colonized the island for France.
After fierce resistance, the last Kalinago defenders reportedly leapt to their deaths at Sauteurs, ending indigenous control.
Grenada became a French crown colony administered as part of the French Antilles.
French colonists expanded plantation agriculture, growing sugar and indigo worked by enslaved Africans.
British forces captured Grenada from France during the Seven Years' War.
The Treaty of Paris formally ceded Grenada to Britain, beginning British colonial rule.
France recaptured Grenada during the American Revolutionary War after assaulting the British garrison.
The Treaty of Versailles returned Grenada to British control.
Julien Fedon, a free mixed-race planter inspired by the French Revolution, led a major slave and free-colored rebellion against British rule.
British forces suppressed Fedon's Rebellion, though Fedon himself was never captured.
Slavery was abolished throughout the British Empire, beginning emancipation in Grenada under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation took effect as the apprenticeship system ended, freeing all enslaved people in Grenada.
Nutmeg was introduced to Grenada, eventually making the island a leading global producer and earning it the nickname the Isle of Spice.
Grenada became a British Crown Colony, ending its earlier representative assembly government.
St. George's, Grenada's capital, became the administrative seat of the Windward Islands colony.
Eric Gairy led a major labor uprising and founded the Grenada United Labour Party, winning the colony's first elections under universal adult suffrage.
Grenada joined the short-lived West Indies Federation.
The West Indies Federation dissolved, ending the attempt at a unified British Caribbean state.
Grenada became an Associated State of the United Kingdom, gaining full internal self-government.
Grenada achieved full independence from Britain on 7 February with Eric Gairy as its first prime minister.
Grenada was admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state on 17 September.
The Marxist New Jewel Movement led by Maurice Bishop seized power in a near-bloodless coup, establishing the People's Revolutionary Government.
A power struggle led to Maurice Bishop's house arrest and his execution by hardline factions of his own party on 19 October.
The United States led an invasion code-named Operation Urgent Fury, overthrowing the military regime and restoring constitutional government.
Elections returned Grenada to democratic civilian rule under Prime Minister Herbert Blaize and the New National Party.
Trials of those responsible for Maurice Bishop's killing concluded with convictions for the Grenada 17.
Keith Mitchell became prime minister, beginning a long period of New National Party dominance in Grenadian politics.
Hurricane Ivan devastated Grenada, damaging or destroying around 90 percent of buildings and crippling the nutmeg industry.
Hurricane Emily struck the island again, compounding the recovery challenges from the previous year's destruction.
The National Democratic Congress under Tillman Thomas won general elections, ousting Keith Mitchell's government.
Point Salines International Airport was renamed Maurice Bishop International Airport, symbolizing national reconciliation.
Keith Mitchell returned to power as his New National Party swept all 15 parliamentary seats.
The New National Party again won all 15 seats, and voters rejected a referendum to replace the UK Privy Council with the Caribbean Court of Justice.
Grenada confirmed its first cases of COVID-19 and imposed restrictions amid the global pandemic.
The National Democratic Congress led by Dickon Mitchell won the general election, ending nearly a decade of Keith Mitchell's rule.
Hurricane Beryl, an exceptionally early Category 4 storm, devastated Grenada's islands of Carriacou and Petite Martinique.
